intro.kicker: Topic 3 of 10 • Chapter: Newton's Laws of Motion • Unit: Mechanics • Physics
intro.description: Linear momentum is the product of a body's mass and its velocity: p = mv. It is a vector quantity with the same direction as velocity. Measured in kg·m/s (SI) or g·cm/s (CGS). Dimension: [MLT⁻¹]. Newton's Second Law is built on the rate of change of linear momentum. NEET frequently tests momentum of bodies with same momentum but different masses, and conservation scenarios.

expectedQuestions.description: Frequently tested in isolation and as a prerequisite for Newton's Second Law, impulse, and conservation of momentum. Expect direct formula-based and comparison MCQs.

timeRequired.description: Cover definition, vector nature, proportionality relations, and numerical comparisons. Practise PYQs on equal-momentum bodies.

difficulty.description: Definition and formula are direct. The trap is applying vector addition of momenta and handling same-momentum/different-mass comparisons correctly.

nriUsaGap.description: US courses cover momentum thoroughly but may not emphasise the CGS vs SI unit comparison or Dimension [MLT⁻¹] as explicitly as NEET does.

bulletNotes: Questions often combine momentum with impulse or collision contexts.
Same-momentum-different-mass comparison (lighter body has greater velocity) is a near-annual NEET question type.
Vector nature of momentum is tested via 2D momentum addition.

strategySteps.title: Anchor the formula and units
strategySteps.description: p = mv; SI unit kg·m/s, CGS g·cm/s, Dimension [MLT⁻¹]. These appear directly in NEET MCQs.

strategySteps.title: Practise same-momentum comparisons
strategySteps.description: If two bodies have the same momentum: v ∝ 1/m — the lighter body is faster. This relation is tested repeatedly.

strategySteps.title: Connect to Newton's 2nd Law
strategySteps.description: F = dp/dt. Momentum is the bridge from kinematics to dynamics. Ensure you can derive F = ma from this.

cards.title: Linear Momentum — Full Notes
cards.description: Complete derivation of p = mv, vector properties, dimensional analysis, and NEET numerical patterns.

cards.title: Momentum Numericals Pack
cards.description: 25 solved numericals on momentum comparison, impulse, and collision setup.

cards.title: Definition and Formula
cards.coreRevision: Linear momentum is the quantity of motion in a body — the product of mass and velocity. p = mv.
points: Vector quantity — direction same as velocity
cards.example: A 2 kg ball moving at 3 m/s east has momentum p = 2 × 3 = 6 kg·m/s east.

cards.title: Same Momentum, Different Mass
cards.coreRevision: If p = m₁v₁ = m₂v₂ (equal momenta), then v₁/v₂ = m₂/m₁. The lighter body has greater velocity.
points: More massive body moves slower for the same momentum
Direct NEET question type — memorise this inverse relationship
cards.example: If a 4 kg body and a 1 kg body have the same momentum, the 1 kg body moves 4× faster.

cards.title: Proportionality Relations
cards.coreRevision: For a given body: p ∝ v (at constant mass). For different bodies at same velocity: p ∝ m.
points: Double the velocity → double the momentum
Double the mass → double the momentum (at same speed)
Momentum is measured by force needed to stop body in unit time
cards.example: A car (1000 kg) at 10 m/s has the same momentum as a truck (500 kg) at 20 m/s only if p₁ = p₂ — always check the numbers.

curriculum.hintText: Key differences between CBSE/NEET and US AP Physics syllabus for linear momentum.

gaps.title: CGS vs SI Unit Emphasis
gaps.description: US AP Physics uses SI exclusively; NEET tests both CGS (g·cm/s) and SI (kg·m/s) unit conversion.
bullets: NEET unit-comparison MCQs require fluency in both systems

gaps.title: Dimensional Formula
gaps.description: US courses rarely test dimensional formula [MLT⁻¹] directly; NEET does.
bullets: Know dimensions of momentum vs impulse vs force

questions.question: Two bodies A and B have masses 5 kg and 10 kg respectively. If they have the same linear momentum, the ratio of their kinetic energies KA : KB is:
questions.explanation: p = mv → v = p/m. KE = p²/(2m). So KE ∝ 1/m when p is constant. KA/KB = mB/mA = 10/5 = 2. Hence KA : KB = 2 : 1.

questions.question: The SI unit of linear momentum is:
questions.explanation: Linear momentum p = mv; unit = kg × m/s = kg·m/s. Note that kg·m/s² = Newton (force), N·m = Joule (energy). Momentum unit is distinct from both.

questions.question: A 3 kg object moves at 4 m/s and a 6 kg object moves at 2 m/s. Compare their momenta.
The dimensional formula for linear momentum is:
For a given body (fixed mass), when velocity doubles, momentum:
A lighter body and a heavier body have the same linear momentum. Which has greater speed?

items.title: Memorise p = mv
items.description: Vector quantity, direction = direction of velocity. SI unit kg·m/s, [MLT⁻¹].

items.title: Know the CGS unit
items.description: g·cm/s; be ready for unit conversion in NEET MCQs.

items.title: Apply same-momentum comparison
items.description: When p is constant, v varies inversely with m. Lighter body moves faster.

items.title: Connect to Newton's 2nd Law
items.description: F = dp/dt is the fundamental form. F = ma is derived for constant mass.

items.title: KE–momentum link
items.description: KE = p²/(2m). At same momentum, lighter body has more KE — common NEET question.

items.question: Is momentum a scalar or vector?
items.answer: Momentum is a vector quantity with the same direction as the velocity of the body. When adding momenta of multiple bodies, you must use vector addition, not simple arithmetic.

items.question: If two bodies have the same speed but different masses, which has more momentum?
items.answer: The heavier body has more momentum. Since p = mv and velocity is the same, p ∝ m. The body with greater mass has proportionally greater momentum.

items.question: How is momentum measured in terms of force?
items.answer: Linear momentum is also measured as the force required to stop the body in unit time. This comes from Newton's 2nd Law: F = dp/dt → p = F × t (impulse). So 1 kg·m/s = 1 N·s.

grounding_summary: Page 189 defines linear momentum as the quantity of motion in a body, measured as the product of mass and velocity (p = mv). It is a vector quantity with SI unit kg-m/sec and CGS unit g-cm/sec, dimension [MLT⁻¹].

Page 189 explicitly states that for bodies with the same momentum, the lighter body possesses greater velocity (v ∝ 1/m), and that for a given body p ∝ v while for different bodies at same velocity p ∝ m.
grounding_excerpt: Linear momentum of a body is the quantity of motion contained in the body.
It is a vector quantity and it's direction is same as the direction of velocity of the body.
If two objects of different masses have same momentum, the lighter body possesses greater velocity.

sources.title: NCERT Physics Part I — Laws of Motion (Chapter 4)
sources.why_used: Primary NCERT source for momentum definition, formula, units, and dimensional analysis.

sources.title: Laws of Motion Detailed Explanation — Physics Class 11 — NEET
sources.why_used: NEET-oriented enrichment on momentum comparisons, KE–momentum relations, and exam MCQ patterns.

review.notes: All content grounded in page 189. No LaTeX in verbatim excerpts.
